column2: What feature makes a deceptive message a phishing attack rather than merely unsolicited email?
column4: It uses deception to make the recipient reveal information, follow a malicious link, or perform another unsafe action.
column5: Spoofed identity or copied branding can make the lure believable, but the human-directed attempt to obtain an action or information is the key.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.5; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 35
column10: A+::220-1202::2.5 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SocialEngineeringThreatsandVulnerabilities Basic HighYield Phishing SocialEngineering Recognition Source::Messer-v140

column2: A caller claiming to represent a bank asks a user to confirm account details. Which phishing variant uses this voice channel?
column5: Caller ID can be spoofed, so the displayed number does not authenticate the caller.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.5; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 35
column10: A+::220-1202::2.5 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SocialEngineeringThreatsandVulnerabilities Basic HighYield Vishing Phishing Voice Source::Messer-v140

column2: A text message claims a package cannot be delivered until the recipient opens a link and confirms personal information. Which phishing variant is this?
column5: The text-message channel distinguishes smishing from email phishing and voice-based vishing.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.5; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 35
column10: A+::220-1202::2.5 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SocialEngineeringThreatsandVulnerabilities Basic HighYield Smishing Phishing SMS Source::Messer-v140

column2: An attacker places a replacement QR-code sticker over a legitimate code so scans open a malicious site. What attack is this?
column5: The code conceals its destination until it is scanned, making destination verification an important defense.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.5; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 35
column10: A+::220-1202::2.5 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SocialEngineeringThreatsandVulnerabilities Basic QRPhishing Phishing Recognition Source::Messer-v140

column2: How does whaling differ from spear phishing?
column4: Spear phishing targets a particular person or group; whaling is spear phishing aimed specifically at a senior executive or other high-value leader.
column5: Both are targeted and may use information about the victim. The target's senior or high-value role is what makes the attack whaling.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.5; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 35
column10: A+::220-1202::2.5 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SocialEngineeringThreatsandVulnerabilities Basic HighYield SpearPhishing Whaling Comparison Source::Messer-v140

column2: What social-engineering attack obtains sensitive information by watching a person's screen or keyboard?
column5: The observer may be nearby or use a camera or other distant viewing method. Screen placement and privacy filters help reduce exposure.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.5; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, pp. 35-36
column10: A+::220-1202::2.5 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SocialEngineeringThreatsandVulnerabilities Basic HighYield ShoulderSurfing PhysicalSecurity Recognition Source::Messer-v140

column2: An unauthorized person slips through a controlled door immediately behind an employee without the employee's consent. What attack occurred?
column5: The approved notes contrast tailgating with piggybacking, where the authorized person knowingly permits the follower to enter. Neither gives the follower independent authorization.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.5; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 36
column10: A+::220-1202::2.5 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SocialEngineeringThreatsandVulnerabilities Basic HighYield Tailgating PhysicalSecurity AccessControl Source::Messer-v140

column2: An attacker claims to be from the help desk and uses technical language to persuade an employee to disclose a code. Which social-engineering method is central to the attack?
column5: The attacker pretends to hold a trusted identity or role. Authority, familiarity, and technical detail are persuasion tools, not proof of identity.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.5; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 36
column10: A+::220-1202::2.5 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SocialEngineeringThreatsandVulnerabilities Basic Impersonation SocialEngineering Trust Source::Messer-v140

column2: What attack searches discarded material for names, schedules, invoices, or other information useful in a later attack?
column5: Discarded fragments can make later phishing or impersonation more convincing even when no password is found.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.5; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 36
column10: A+::220-1202::2.5 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SocialEngineeringThreatsandVulnerabilities Basic DumpsterDiving SocialEngineering InformationGathering Source::Messer-v140

column2: What distinguishes a distributed denial-of-service attack from a general denial-of-service attack?
column4: A DDoS attack coordinates the disruption from many systems, while a DoS attack does not require multiple distributed sources.
column5: Both target availability. DDoS commonly uses a botnet to exhaust bandwidth or service resources from many origins at once.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.5; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 36
column10: A+::220-1202::2.5 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SocialEngineeringThreatsandVulnerabilities Basic HighYield DoS DDoS Availability Comparison Source::Messer-v140

column2: A malicious access point uses the same SSID and a similar captive portal as a trusted hotspot. What threat does this describe?
column5: The access point imitates a legitimate network to attract users. A familiar SSID alone does not authenticate the wireless network.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.5; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 37
column10: A+::220-1202::2.5 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SocialEngineeringThreatsandVulnerabilities Basic HighYield EvilTwin WirelessSecurity Recognition Source::Messer-v140

column2: What makes an attack a zero-day attack?
column4: It exploits a vulnerability before that vulnerability has been detected or publicly disclosed.
column5: Attackers may keep an unknown flaw private so they can use it before defenders publish details and begin remediation.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.5; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 38
column10: A+::220-1202::2.5 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SocialEngineeringThreatsandVulnerabilities Basic HighYield ZeroDay Vulnerabilities Recognition Source::Messer-v140

column2: How does spoofing differ from an on-path attack?
column4: Spoofing falsifies an identity or source. An on-path attack intercepts and relays communication between two parties, potentially reading or changing it.
column5: The techniques can work together: ARP spoofing can redirect local traffic through an attacker and thereby establish an on-path position.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.5; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 37
column10: A+::220-1202::2.5 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SocialEngineeringThreatsandVulnerabilities Basic HighYield Spoofing OnPath Comparison Source::Messer-v140

column2: How does a dictionary password attack differ from a brute-force password attack?
column4: A dictionary attack tries likely words and human-created patterns; brute force systematically tries the possible password combinations.
column5: Dictionary attacks reduce the search space by exploiting predictable password choices. Brute force is broader and may require much more time or computing power.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.5; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 38
column10: A+::220-1202::2.5 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SocialEngineeringThreatsandVulnerabilities Basic HighYield BruteForce DictionaryAttack Passwords Comparison Source::Messer-v140

column2: Why can an insider threat be especially difficult to identify and contain?
column4: The insider may already have trusted access and knowledge of valuable systems and normal organizational processes.
column5: An insider may be an employee, contractor, or recruited trusted person. The defining advantage is legitimate access or institutional knowledge, not advanced technical skill.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.5; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 38
column10: A+::220-1202::2.5 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SocialEngineeringThreatsandVulnerabilities Basic HighYield InsiderThreat AccessControl Recognition Source::Messer-v140

column2: An application inserts untrusted input directly into a database query, allowing the input to alter the query's meaning. What attack is this?
column5: The application failed to keep user-supplied data separate from database instructions. The manipulated query can expose or change database information.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.5; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 39
column10: A+::220-1202::2.5 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SocialEngineeringThreatsandVulnerabilities Basic HighYield SQLInjection ApplicationSecurity InputValidation Source::Messer-v140

column2: A website accepts attacker-supplied script that later executes in a visitor's browser as though it came from the trusted site. What attack is this?
column5: XSS targets the browser and the user's trust in a site. This differs from SQL injection, which changes a query sent to a database.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.5; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 39
column10: A+::220-1202::2.5 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SocialEngineeringThreatsandVulnerabilities Basic HighYield XSS ApplicationSecurity InputValidation Source::Messer-v140

column2: An urgent email that appears to come from an executive directs accounting to send payment to a new bank account. What threat should be suspected?
column5: BEC abuses trust in business communications, often through spoofing, compromised accounts, or targeted social engineering. Verify sensitive requests through a separate trusted channel.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.5; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 40
column10: A+::220-1202::2.5 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SocialEngineeringThreatsandVulnerabilities Basic HighYield BEC EmailSecurity SocialEngineering Source::Messer-v140

column2: Attackers compromise a vendor's trusted software-update process so malicious code reaches the vendor's customers. What type of attack is this?
column5: The attacker reaches downstream organizations through something they trust from a supplier or provider. This differs from an insider acting from within the target organization.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.5; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, pp. 40-41
column10: A+::220-1202::2.5 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SocialEngineeringThreatsandVulnerabilities Basic HighYield SupplyChain ThirdPartyRisk Trust Source::Messer-v140

column2: What makes a system non-compliant?
column4: It does not meet the organization's required security baseline or policy.
column5: A non-compliant system might have unauthorized software, missing updates, or a configuration outside the approved standard operating environment.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.5; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 41
column10: A+::220-1202::2.5 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SocialEngineeringThreatsandVulnerabilities Basic Compliance Baselines Vulnerabilities Source::Messer-v140

column2: Why is an unpatched system a security vulnerability?
column4: It lacks available fixes, leaving known operating-system or application flaws open to exploitation.
column5: Patch management includes identifying, testing, prioritizing, and deploying updates across all systems, including easily overlooked devices.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.5; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 41
column10: A+::220-1202::2.5 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SocialEngineeringThreatsandVulnerabilities Basic HighYield Patching Vulnerabilities Maintenance Source::Messer-v140

column2: What makes a system unprotected in the context of Objective 2.5?
column4: An expected security control, such as antivirus or a firewall, is missing or disabled.
column5: Permanently removing a control to solve an application problem creates a new exposure. Troubleshoot the compatibility or configuration issue instead.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.5; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 41
column10: A+::220-1202::2.5 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SocialEngineeringThreatsandVulnerabilities Basic HighYield SecurityControls Antivirus Firewall Vulnerabilities Source::Messer-v140

column2: What security risk becomes critical when technology passes the point at which its vendor provides support and security updates?
column4: Newly discovered vulnerabilities may remain unpatched because the vendor no longer supplies fixes.
column5: The objective labels the lifecycle concern EOL. The approved notes distinguish end of sales from the later end of service life; loss of security updates is the decisive security issue.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.5; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 41
column10: A+::220-1202::2.5 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SocialEngineeringThreatsandVulnerabilities Basic HighYield EOL Lifecycle Patching Vulnerabilities Source::Messer-v140

column2: Why does bring your own device create a security challenge for an organization?
column4: An employee-owned device mixes personal and work use, complicating security control, malware exposure, company-data protection, and data removal.
column5: The organization must protect its data while the employee owns and also uses the device personally, including when the device is lost, sold, or replaced.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.5; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 41
column10: A+::220-1202::2.5 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SocialEngineeringThreatsandVulnerabilities Basic HighYield BYOD MobileSecurity DataProtection Source::Messer-v140
